flashcards.back: Linear Search (or Sequential Search) is a simple searching algorithm that checks each element in a list/array one by one until the target element is found or the entire list is exhausted.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Linear Search in the worst case?
flashcards.back: O(n) - In the worst case, the algorithm must check every element before finding the target (or determining it doesn't exist).

flashcards.front: What is the best case time complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(1) - Best case occurs when the target element is found at the first position.

flashcards.front: What is the average case time complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(n) - On average, the element is found at the middle position, requiring n/2 comparisons, which simplifies to O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(1) - Linear Search uses only a constant amount of extra space regardless of input size.

flashcards.front: When is Linear Search preferred over Binary Search?
flashcards.back: Linear Search is preferred when: 1) The array is unsorted, 2) The array is small, 3) Searching is done only once, or 4) The data structure doesn't support random access.

flashcards.front: Can Linear Search be used on sorted arrays?
flashcards.back: Yes, but it's inefficient compared to Binary Search. It can be optimized to stop early if the current element exceeds the target in a sorted array.
A variation where the target element is appended at the end of the array to eliminate the boundary check in each iteration, reducing comparisons slightly.

flashcards.front: What is the key disadvantage of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: It has O(n) time complexity, making it inefficient for large datasets compared to O(log n) algorithms like Binary Search.

flashcards.front: How does Linear Search handle duplicate elements?
flashcards.back: Standard Linear Search returns the first occurrence of the target. To find all occurrences, the algorithm must continue searching after finding a match.

flashcards.front: Can Linear Search be implemented recursively?
flashcards.back: Yes, but it requires O(n) stack space for recursion. Iterative implementation is generally preferred for its O(1) space complexity.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of comparisons in Linear Search?
flashcards.back: 1 comparison - When the target element is at the first position (best case).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum number of comparisons in Linear Search?
flashcards.back: n comparisons - When the target element is at the last position or not present in the array (worst case).
Yes, Linear Search is stable as it doesn't change the relative order of equal elements. It finds the first occurrence.

flashcards.front: What data structures can Linear Search be applied to?
flashcards.back: Any linear data structure: arrays, linked lists, files, or any collection where elements can be accessed sequentially.
